domain: Multilingual guitar education, world music traditions, and cross-cultural musical pedagogy in 10 languages
research_areas: Multilingual guitar pedagogy — localized curriculum for 10 languages
Musical terminology mapping across languages and traditions
World guitar traditions — regional styles, tunings, techniques
Cross-cultural music theory — scales, modes, and tonal systems from diverse traditions
Chatbot multilingual interaction — natural language pedagogy in 10 languages
Cultural context in music education — idioms, metaphors, teaching styles by culture
Translation quality and musical accuracy — avoiding false cognates in music terms
Regional notation conventions — Nashville numbers, cifra (Brazil), jianpu (China)

supported_languages.name: Mandarin Chinese
supported_languages.notable_terminology: Jianpu (numbered notation 简谱), pentatonic focus (五声音阶)
supported_languages.curriculum_notes: Chinese music education emphasizes numbered notation; bridge to Western staff notation

supported_languages.name: Hindi
supported_languages.curriculum_notes: Indian music uses microtonal intervals (shrutis); guitar fret limitations need addressing

supported_languages.name: Spanish
supported_languages.curriculum_notes: Flamenco is one of the richest guitar traditions — deserves deep coverage

supported_languages.name: French
supported_languages.curriculum_notes: Gypsy jazz is a cornerstone French guitar tradition with unique techniques

supported_languages.name: Arabic
supported_languages.curriculum_notes: Arabic music uses quarter-tones not available on standard guitar frets; microtonal guitars or bending technique required

supported_languages.name: Bengali
supported_languages.curriculum_notes: Rich vocal tradition; guitar pedagogy focuses on accompaniment and song-based learning

supported_languages.name: Portuguese
supported_languages.notable_terminology: Violão (guitar), cifra (chord chart notation), batida (strum pattern), baixaria (bass line runs)
supported_languages.curriculum_notes: Brazilian guitar (violão) is arguably the world's richest guitar tradition after flamenco — cifra notation is standard

supported_languages.name: Russian
supported_languages.curriculum_notes: Russian guitar tradition uses 7-string open-G tuning (DGBDGBD) — unique pedagogy needed

supported_languages.name: Japanese
supported_languages.notable_terminology: コード (chord/kōdo), スケール (scale/sukēru), 運指 (fingering/unshi), TAB譜 (tablature)
supported_languages.curriculum_notes: Japanese guitar culture is highly developed; tab-based learning is dominant; katakana music terminology

curriculum: Multilingual Theory: Music terminology in 10 languages — comparative glossary
World Guitar Traditions Survey: One tradition per language, history + technique
Flamenco Foundations (Spanish): Compás, rasgueado, picado, palos
Bossa Nova & Brazilian Guitar (Portuguese): Batidas, harmony, João Gilberto style
Gypsy Jazz Essentials (French): La pompe, Django voicings, diminished runs
Maqam Guitar (Arabic): Quarter-tone bending, taqsim improvisation, Arabic scales
Raga Guitar (Hindi): Alap-style improvisation, Indian scales on fretboard, slide technique
Russian 7-String Guitar: Open-G tuning, romance repertoire, bard accompaniment
Japanese Fingerstyle: Percussive technique, anime/game arrangements, Oshio school
Chinese Guitar: Pentatonic mastery, jianpu reading, C-pop arrangement
Bengali Song Guitar: Tagore songs, Baul accompaniment, Bengali rock
Cross-Cultural Jam: Playing together across traditions — finding common ground
Chatbot Localization: Teaching patterns adapted per language and cultural context
Notation Systems: Staff, tab, cifra, jianpu, Nashville numbers — reading and converting

learning_path_philosophy.principle: Music is the universal language, but it speaks with many accents
learning_path_philosophy.approach: Learn the guitar tradition of each culture alongside the language — music and language reinforce each other
learning_path_philosophy.goal: A GA user in any of the 10 languages can learn guitar through their own musical heritage, then bridge to other traditions
